preamble: Labial palatalization, the shift from a voiceless labial stop [p] to a voiceless palatal stop [c], is a cross-linguistically rarer process but well-documented in several language families. This typically occurs when a labial consonant is followed by a high front glide [j] or vowel [i], leading to the loss of labial articulation and the emergence of a palatal stop. This is found in Bantu languages, specific dialects of Greek, and Southern Italian.

examples.note: Many Bantu languages exhibit palatalization of labials before high front glides (e.g., passive formations), where /p/ + /i/ or /j/ shifts to [c].
In some Greek dialects (e.g., Lesbos), /pj/ becomes [cc] or [c], such as 'spitia' > [stica].
The Latin cluster /pj/ often develops into a palatal stop in Southern Italian varieties.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
